Journal: Knowledge-Based Systems

DOI: 10.1016/j.knosys.2026.115990

Relevance: 0.774 (must_read)

Domain adaptation (DA) techniques have proven effective in cross-domain fault diagnosis; however, their success often relies on the closed-set domain adaptation (CDA) assumption, which is seldom applicable in industrial environments. In the era of industrial big data, there is a growing need to transfer deep learning (DL) models from well-established, large-scale domains to smaller, less familiar ones. To address this challenge, this study introduces a more practical partial-set domain adaptation (PDA) approach called the scale-aware weighting and entropy-minimized ambiguity adversarial domain adaptation (SEADA) network. SEADA incorporates a scale-aware weighting mechanism to assess data reliability across multiple scales, mitigating the adverse effects of outlier classes and improving the model's transferability. Additionally, by employing entropy-minimized ambiguity strategy, SEADA reduces uncertainty in domain instances, enhances inter-class separability, and improves model robustness. The proposed SEADA enables efficient transfer by aligning feature distributions within the shared label space while mitigating the negative influence of outlier classes and ambiguous information during diagnostic transfer. Experimental results demonstrate that the SEADA significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in partial transfer within identical machines (PTIM) and across different machines (PTDM), underscoring its effectiveness in addressing mechanical system challenges.

item.title: Observe Less, Understand More: Cost-aware Cross-scale Observation for Remote Sensing Understanding
item.dc:creator: Zhenghao Xie
Jing Xiao
Zhenqi Wang
Kexin Ma
Liang Liao
Gui-Song Xia
Mi Wang
item.description: Remote sensing understanding inherently requires multi-resolution observation, since different targets and application tasks demand different levels of spatial detail. While low-resolution (LR) imagery enables efficient global observation, high-resolution (HR) imagery provides critical local details at much higher acquisition cost and limited coverage. This motivates a cross-scale sensing strategy that selectively acquires HR imagery from LR-based global perception to improve task performance under constrained cost. Existing methods for HR sampling methods typically make selection decisions from isolated LR patches, which ignore fine-grained intra-patch importance and cross-patch contextual interactions, leading to fragmented feature representation and suboptimal scene reasoning under sparse HR observations. To address this issue, we formulate cross-scale remote sensing understanding as a unified cost-aware problem that couples fine-grained HR sampling with cross-patch representation prediction, enabling more effective task reasoning with fewer HR observations. Furthermore, we present GL-10M, a large-scale benchmark of 10 million spatially aligned multi-resolution images, enabling systematic evaluation of budget-constrained cross-scale reasoning in remote sensing. Extensive experiments on recognition and retrieval tasks show that our method consistently achieves a superior performance-cost trade-off. Published: 2026-04-13T13:00:29+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.773 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zhenghao Xie; Jing Xiao; Zhenqi Wang; Kexin Ma; Liang Liao; Gui-Song Xia; Mi Wang

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.773 (must_read)

Remote sensing understanding inherently requires multi-resolution observation, since different targets and application tasks demand different levels of spatial detail. While low-resolution (LR) imagery enables efficient global observation, high-resolution (HR) imagery provides critical local details at much higher acquisition cost and limited coverage. This motivates a cross-scale sensing strategy that selectively acquires HR imagery from LR-based global perception to improve task performance under constrained cost. Existing methods for HR sampling methods typically make selection decisions from isolated LR patches, which ignore fine-grained intra-patch importance and cross-patch contextual interactions, leading to fragmented feature representation and suboptimal scene reasoning under sparse HR observations. To address this issue, we formulate cross-scale remote sensing understanding as a unified cost-aware problem that couples fine-grained HR sampling with cross-patch representation prediction, enabling more effective task reasoning with fewer HR observations. Furthermore, we present GL-10M, a large-scale benchmark of 10 million spatially aligned multi-resolution images, enabling systematic evaluation of budget-constrained cross-scale reasoning in remote sensing. Extensive experiments on recognition and retrieval tasks show that our method consistently achieves a superior performance-cost trade-off.

item.title: Revisiting Compositionality in Dual-Encoder Vision-Language Models: The Role of Inference
item.dc:creator: Imanol Miranda
Ander Salaberria
Eneko Agirre
Gorka Azkune
item.description: Dual-encoder Vision-Language Models (VLMs) such as CLIP are often characterized as bag-of-words systems due to their poor performance on compositional benchmarks. We argue that this limitation may stem less from deficient representations than from the standard inference protocol based on global cosine similarity. First, through controlled diagnostic experiments, we show that explicitly enforcing fine-grained region-segment alignment at inference dramatically improves compositional performance without updating pretrained encoders. We then introduce a lightweight transformer that learns such alignments directly from frozen patch and token embeddings. Comparing against full fine-tuning and prior end-to-end compositional training methods, we find that although these approaches improve in-domain retrieval, their gains do not consistently transfer under distribution shift. In contrast, learning localized alignment over frozen representations matches full fine-tuning on in-domain retrieval while yielding substantial improvements on controlled out-of-domain compositional benchmarks. These results identify global embedding matching as a key bottleneck in dual-encoder VLMs and highlight the importance of alignment mechanisms for robust compositional generalization. Published: 2026-04-13T14:03:18+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.773 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Imanol Miranda; Ander Salaberria; Eneko Agirre; Gorka Azkune

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.773 (must_read)

Dual-encoder Vision-Language Models (VLMs) such as CLIP are often characterized as bag-of-words systems due to their poor performance on compositional benchmarks. We argue that this limitation may stem less from deficient representations than from the standard inference protocol based on global cosine similarity. First, through controlled diagnostic experiments, we show that explicitly enforcing fine-grained region-segment alignment at inference dramatically improves compositional performance without updating pretrained encoders. We then introduce a lightweight transformer that learns such alignments directly from frozen patch and token embeddings. Comparing against full fine-tuning and prior end-to-end compositional training methods, we find that although these approaches improve in-domain retrieval, their gains do not consistently transfer under distribution shift. In contrast, learning localized alignment over frozen representations matches full fine-tuning on in-domain retrieval while yielding substantial improvements on controlled out-of-domain compositional benchmarks. These results identify global embedding matching as a key bottleneck in dual-encoder VLMs and highlight the importance of alignment mechanisms for robust compositional generalization.

item.title: Large-scale multimodal remote sensing image registration with semantic guidance and multi-scale contextual matching
item.dc:creator: Chang Liu
Haigang Sui
Mingting Zhou
Chuan Xu
item.description: Large-scale multimodal image registration aligns and integrates multi-source remote sensing data for use in diverse applications, but traditional hand-crafted and learning-based methods often struggle in areas with complex terrain and weak image features. Particularly in large-scale remote sensing images containing extensive forests, mountainous regions, or low-texture surfaces, many existing approaches fail to maintain accuracy and timely processing due to severe geometric and radiometric distortions as well as poor feature correspondence. To address these challenges, we propose a high-precision, high-efficiency registration framework called Large-scale multimodal remote sensing image registration with semantic guidance and multi-scale contextual matching (SemReg). This framework adopts a coarse-to-fine strategy to balance computational efficiency with registration accuracy at scale. In the coarse registration stage, we introduce a multi-granularity contextual feature-fusion module that bridges significant inter-modal differences and establishes robust feature correspondences. In the fine registration stage, we employ a Differentiable Spatial-to-Numerical Transform (DSNT) pixel-regression model to achieve refined matching at a granular level. Additionally, a semantically-guided sparse-region matching module leverages semantic dissimilarities to further refine matching results in structurally complex or visually ambiguous areas. Experiments on four benchmark datasets show that SemReg outperforms state-of-the-art deep-learning methods in registration accuracy while also achieving superior generalization. Furthermore, tests on large-format optical, SAR, and multispectral remote sensing images confirm that our approach delivers exceptional matching accuracy with high efficiency and full automation, making it well-suited for operational large-scale applications. Published: 2026-04-13T10:08:04+00:00 Venue: Expert Systems with Applications Score: 0.772 (must_read)

Authors: Chang Liu; Haigang Sui; Mingting Zhou; Chuan Xu

Journal: Expert Systems with Applications

DOI: 10.1016/j.eswa.2026.132455

Relevance: 0.772 (must_read)

Large-scale multimodal image registration aligns and integrates multi-source remote sensing data for use in diverse applications, but traditional hand-crafted and learning-based methods often struggle in areas with complex terrain and weak image features. Particularly in large-scale remote sensing images containing extensive forests, mountainous regions, or low-texture surfaces, many existing approaches fail to maintain accuracy and timely processing due to severe geometric and radiometric distortions as well as poor feature correspondence. To address these challenges, we propose a high-precision, high-efficiency registration framework called Large-scale multimodal remote sensing image registration with semantic guidance and multi-scale contextual matching (SemReg). This framework adopts a coarse-to-fine strategy to balance computational efficiency with registration accuracy at scale. In the coarse registration stage, we introduce a multi-granularity contextual feature-fusion module that bridges significant inter-modal differences and establishes robust feature correspondences. In the fine registration stage, we employ a Differentiable Spatial-to-Numerical Transform (DSNT) pixel-regression model to achieve refined matching at a granular level. Additionally, a semantically-guided sparse-region matching module leverages semantic dissimilarities to further refine matching results in structurally complex or visually ambiguous areas. Experiments on four benchmark datasets show that SemReg outperforms state-of-the-art deep-learning methods in registration accuracy while also achieving superior generalization. Furthermore, tests on large-format optical, SAR, and multispectral remote sensing images confirm that our approach delivers exceptional matching accuracy with high efficiency and full automation, making it well-suited for operational large-scale applications.

item.title: PAS: Estimating the target accuracy before domain adaptation
item.dc:creator: Raphaella Diniz
Jackson de Faria
Martin Ester
item.description: The goal of domain adaptation is to make predictions for unlabeled samples from a target domain with the help of labeled samples from a different but related source domain. The performance of domain adaptation methods is highly influenced by the choice of source domain and pre-trained feature extractor. However, the selection of source data and pre-trained model is not trivial due to the absence of a labeled validation set for the target domain and the large number of available pre-trained models. In this work, we propose PAS, a novel score designed to estimate the transferability of a source domain set and a pre-trained feature extractor to a target classification task before actually performing domain adaptation. PAS leverages the generalization power of pre-trained models and assesses source-target compatibility based on the pre-trained feature embeddings. We integrate PAS into a framework that indicates the most relevant pre-trained model and source domain among multiple candidates, thus improving target accuracy while reducing the computational overhead. Extensive experiments on image classification benchmarks demonstrate that PAS correlates strongly with actual target accuracy and consistently guides the selection of the best-performing pre-trained model and source domain for adaptation. Published: 2026-04-10T19:46:19+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.771 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Raphaella Diniz; Jackson de Faria; Martin Ester

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.771 (must_read)

The goal of domain adaptation is to make predictions for unlabeled samples from a target domain with the help of labeled samples from a different but related source domain. The performance of domain adaptation methods is highly influenced by the choice of source domain and pre-trained feature extractor. However, the selection of source data and pre-trained model is not trivial due to the absence of a labeled validation set for the target domain and the large number of available pre-trained models. In this work, we propose PAS, a novel score designed to estimate the transferability of a source domain set and a pre-trained feature extractor to a target classification task before actually performing domain adaptation. PAS leverages the generalization power of pre-trained models and assesses source-target compatibility based on the pre-trained feature embeddings. We integrate PAS into a framework that indicates the most relevant pre-trained model and source domain among multiple candidates, thus improving target accuracy while reducing the computational overhead. Extensive experiments on image classification benchmarks demonstrate that PAS correlates strongly with actual target accuracy and consistently guides the selection of the best-performing pre-trained model and source domain for adaptation.

item.title: Semantic-Geometric Dual Compression: Training-Free Visual Token Reduction for Ultra-High-Resolution Remote Sensing Understanding
item.dc:creator: Yueying Li
Fengxiang Wang
Yan Li
Mingshuo Chen
Mengying Zhao
Long Lan
item.description: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated immense potential in Earth observation. However, the massive visual tokens generated when processing Ultra-High-Resolution (UHR) imagery introduce prohibitive computational overhead, severely bottlenecking their inference efficiency. Existing visual token compression methods predominantly adopt static and uniform compression strategies, neglecting the inherent "Semantic-Geometric Duality" in remote sensing interpretation tasks. Specifically, object semantic tasks focus on the abstract semantics of objects and benefit from aggressive background pruning, whereas scene geometric tasks critically rely on the integrity of spatial topology. To address this challenge, we propose DualComp, a task-adaptive dual-stream token compression framework. Dynamically guided by a lightweight pre-trained router, DualComp decouples feature processing into two dedicated pathways. In the object semantic stream, the Spatially-Contiguous Semantic Aggregator (SCSA) utilizes size-adaptive clustering to aggregates redundant background while protecting small object. In the scene geometric stream, the Instruction-Guided Structure Recoverer (IGSR) introduces a greedy path-tracing topology completion mechanism to reconstruct spatial skeletons. Experiments on the UHR remote sensing benchmark XLRS-Bench demonstrate that DualComp accomplishes high-fidelity remote sensing interpretation at an exceptionally low computational cost, achieving simultaneous improvements in both efficiency and accuracy. Published: 2026-04-13T07:36:02+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.771 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yueying Li; Fengxiang Wang; Yan Li; Mingshuo Chen; Mengying Zhao; Long Lan

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.771 (must_read)

Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated immense potential in Earth observation. However, the massive visual tokens generated when processing Ultra-High-Resolution (UHR) imagery introduce prohibitive computational overhead, severely bottlenecking their inference efficiency. Existing visual token compression methods predominantly adopt static and uniform compression strategies, neglecting the inherent "Semantic-Geometric Duality" in remote sensing interpretation tasks. Specifically, object semantic tasks focus on the abstract semantics of objects and benefit from aggressive background pruning, whereas scene geometric tasks critically rely on the integrity of spatial topology. To address this challenge, we propose DualComp, a task-adaptive dual-stream token compression framework. Dynamically guided by a lightweight pre-trained router, DualComp decouples feature processing into two dedicated pathways. In the object semantic stream, the Spatially-Contiguous Semantic Aggregator (SCSA) utilizes size-adaptive clustering to aggregates redundant background while protecting small object. In the scene geometric stream, the Instruction-Guided Structure Recoverer (IGSR) introduces a greedy path-tracing topology completion mechanism to reconstruct spatial skeletons. Experiments on the UHR remote sensing benchmark XLRS-Bench demonstrate that DualComp accomplishes high-fidelity remote sensing interpretation at an exceptionally low computational cost, achieving simultaneous improvements in both efficiency and accuracy.

item.title: ZoomR: Memory Efficient Reasoning through Multi-Granularity Key Value Retrieval
item.dc:creator: David H. Yang
Yuxuan Zhu
Mohammad Mohammadi Amiri
Keerthiram Murugesan
Tejaswini Pedapati
Subhajit Chaudhury
Pin-Yu Chen
item.description: Large language models (LLMs) have shown great performance on complex reasoning tasks but often require generating long intermediate thoughts before reaching a final answer. During generation, LLMs rely on a key-value (KV) cache for autoregressive decoding. However, the memory footprint of the KV cache grows with output length. Prior work on KV cache optimization mostly focus on compressing the long input context, while retaining the full KV cache for decoding. For tasks requiring long output generation, this leads to increased computational and memory costs. In this paper, we introduce ZoomR, a novel approach that enables LLMs to adaptively compress verbose reasoning thoughts into summaries and uses a dynamic KV cache selection policy that leverages these summaries while also strategically "zooming in" on fine-grained details. By using summary keys as a coarse-grained index during decoding, ZoomR uses the query to retrieve details for only the most important thoughts. This hierarchical strategy significantly reduces memory usage by avoiding full-cache attention at each step. Experiments across math and reasoning tasks show that our approach achieves competitive performance compared to baselines, while reducing inference memory requirements by more than $4\times$. These results demonstrate that a multi-granularity KV selection enables more memory efficient decoding, especially for long output generation. Published: 2026-04-13T02:00:35+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.771 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: David H. Yang; Yuxuan Zhu; Mohammad Mohammadi Amiri; Keerthiram Murugesan; Tejaswini Pedapati; Subhajit Chaudhury; Pin-Yu Chen

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.771 (must_read)

Large language models (LLMs) have shown great performance on complex reasoning tasks but often require generating long intermediate thoughts before reaching a final answer. During generation, LLMs rely on a key-value (KV) cache for autoregressive decoding. However, the memory footprint of the KV cache grows with output length. Prior work on KV cache optimization mostly focus on compressing the long input context, while retaining the full KV cache for decoding. For tasks requiring long output generation, this leads to increased computational and memory costs. In this paper, we introduce ZoomR, a novel approach that enables LLMs to adaptively compress verbose reasoning thoughts into summaries and uses a dynamic KV cache selection policy that leverages these summaries while also strategically "zooming in" on fine-grained details. By using summary keys as a coarse-grained index during decoding, ZoomR uses the query to retrieve details for only the most important thoughts. This hierarchical strategy significantly reduces memory usage by avoiding full-cache attention at each step. Experiments across math and reasoning tasks show that our approach achieves competitive performance compared to baselines, while reducing inference memory requirements by more than $4\times$. These results demonstrate that a multi-granularity KV selection enables more memory efficient decoding, especially for long output generation.
